description: Server-side DOM implementation based on Mozilla's dom.js

vulnerabilities.description: outerHTML setter parses arbitrary HTML via mozHTMLParser without sanitization. Attacker-controlled HTML passed to element.outerHTML = '...' is parsed into the DOM tree including <script>, <iframe>, and event handler attributes (onclick, onerror, etc.). If the resulting DOM is serialized (via innerHTML/outerHTML getter) and served to browsers, stored XSS is possible.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Add HTML sanitization before parsing, or document that consumers must sanitize output. Consider stripping <script>, <iframe>, <object>, <embed> tags and event handler attributes from parsed content.

vulnerabilities.description: innerHTML setter on HTMLElement parses arbitrary HTML via mozHTMLParser without sanitization. Same risk as outerHTML setter — untrusted HTML is parsed into DOM without any filtering of dangerous elements or attributes.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Same as above: sanitize parsed HTML or document consumer responsibility.

vulnerabilities.description: dangerouslySetInnerHTML setter exists and parses HTML without sanitization. Name acknowledges danger (mirrors React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML) but provides no built-in protection. Acts as passthrough to innerHTML setter.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Document that this is intentionally dangerous. Add JSDoc warning about XSS risks when output is served to browsers.

vulnerabilities.description: Event constructor copies all enumerable properties from dictionary argument via for...in loop: `this[p] = dictionary[p]`. While __proto__ is typically non-enumerable, this pattern is fragile. If combined with Object.prototype pollution elsewhere, could allow property injection into Event instances.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Use Object.keys(dictionary) instead of for...in, or use Object.defineProperties with explicit property whitelist for known Event properties.

vulnerabilities.description: insertAdjacentHTML implementation parses arbitrary HTML via mozHTMLParser without sanitization. Accepts position+text and parses the text as HTML, inserting into DOM at specified position. Same XSS risk profile as innerHTML/outerHTML.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Sanitize parsed HTML fragment before insertion.

vulnerabilities.description: HTMLParser is a full HTML5 parser (~7000 lines) that processes all HTML elements including <script> (HTMLScriptElement defined at line 977), <iframe>, <object>, event handler attributes, and javascript: URLs. No built-in sanitization or content security filtering. Library is server-side so scripts don't execute, but parsed malicious content persists in DOM and can be serialized back to HTML.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Add optional sanitization mode to HTMLParser. Provide a configuration option to strip dangerous elements/attributes during parsing.

xssAndInjection.note: Server-side HTML parsing without sanitization; risk depends on consumer usage

other.note: Event prototype pollution minor risk; HTMLParser complexity surface

positiveFindings: Zero production dependencies — no supply chain attack surface from npm packages
No hardcoded secrets, API keys, passwords, or tokens found in any source file
No eval(), new Function(), or dynamic code execution found
No filesystem access (fs module not imported)
No child_process, shell execution, or network calls
Element.prototype.innerHTML setter correctly marked as utils.nyi (not implemented) at base class level
HTMLParser uses __proto__: null objects for lookups (null-prototype safety)
Library is server-side only — scripts are parsed as DOM nodes but never executed
dangerouslySetInnerHTML name explicitly signals danger to consumers
riskContext: Domino is a server-side DOM library. It has ZERO attack surface from dependencies (no production deps). The primary risk is misuse: if consumer applications parse untrusted HTML via innerHTML/outerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML setters and then serialize the DOM back to HTML served to browsers without sanitization, stored/persistent XSS is possible. The library itself does not execute scripts, access the filesystem, or make network calls. Risk score 22/100 reflects low inherent risk with medium risk contingent on consumer usage patterns.
recommendations: DOCUMENT: Add prominent security warning in README that innerHTML/outerHTML/insertAdjacentHTML parse arbitrary HTML without sanitization. Consumers MUST sanitize output before serving to browsers.
SANITIZE: Add optional HTML sanitization mode to HTMLParser that strips <script>, <iframe>, <object>, <embed> tags and event handler attributes (on*=) when enabled.
HARDEN: Replace for...in loop in Event constructor (Event.js:30) with Object.keys(dictionary).forEach() to avoid prototype pollution risk.
HARDEN: Consider adding a configurable allowlist/blocklist for HTML elements and attributes during parsing.
TEST: Add security test cases verifying that script tags are parsed but marked/flagged appropriately, and that serialization can be configured to exclude dangerous content.
DEPENDENCY: Monitor devDependencies (mocha@^6.2.3, puppeteer@^21.3.5) for vulnerabilities. Though dev-only, they're present in developer environments.
USAGE: If using domino in a web-facing service, ALWAYS run parsed/serialized HTML through a sanitizer (e.g., DOMPurify on the server side) before sending to clients.
